title: Listening - Part 3: Seeing is No Longer Believing
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with an art critic, Abbi, and a computer scientist, Elliot, about the impact of deepfake technology on society. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding the 'Liar’s Dividend' and the erosion of shared truth in the digital age.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: If you can’t trust your eyes, what can you trust? Deepfakes—AI-generated videos that look incredibly real—are everywhere. Joining me are Abbi, a cultural critic, and Elliot, a specialist in AI ethics. Abbi, is this the end of 'objective truth'?

Abbi: [15] We’ve always had propaganda, but this is a difference of scale. Before, you needed a Hollywood studio to fake a video; now, a teenager with a laptop can do it. [/15] The real danger isn't just that we believe false things, but what we call the 'Liar’s Dividend.' This is when a politician is caught doing something wrong on real video, and they simply claim it’s a deepfake. It allows people in power to escape accountability by casting doubt on reality itself.

Interviewer: Elliot, you’re working on 'detection' software. Are we winning the arms race against the fakes?

Elliot: [16] It’s a constant back-and-forth. As soon as we find a way to detect a fake—for example, by looking at how a person blinks—the AI is updated to blink more realistically. [/16] We are moving toward 'cryptographic signatures.' Imagine every camera having a unique digital fingerprint that marks a video as 'authentic' the moment it’s recorded. But that requires a level of global cooperation we haven't seen yet.

Interviewer: Abbi, there’s also a positive side. We’re seeing deepfakes used in education and film. Is that okay?

Abbi: [17] It’s a slippery slope. Using AI to make a historical figure 'speak' to students is engaging, but it’s still a fiction. We are projecting our modern values onto the past. [/17] In film, 'de-aging' actors or bringing back dead stars raises massive ethical questions about consent. Who owns your face after you die? Your family? The studio? We haven't figured that out yet.

Interviewer: Elliot, what about 'scamming'? This isn't just about politics.

Elliot: [18] That’s the most immediate threat. We’re seeing 'vishing'—voice phishing—where a person receives a call from their boss or a relative asking for money, and it sounds exactly like them. [/18] The psychological impact of being betrayed by a familiar voice is devastating. It destroys the social trust that holds communities together.

Interviewer: Abbi, how do we educate the public to be more skeptical without becoming cynical?

Abbi: [19] We need 'digital literacy' in schools, starting very young. We have to teach people to look for 'contextual clues' rather than just visual ones. [/19] Does this video fit with what we know about this person? Who is sharing it? What is their motive? We have to stop being passive consumers and start being active investigators.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Elliot? Can we ever go back to a world where a video was proof?

Elliot: [20] No. That era is over. We have to treat digital information the same way we treat a rumor—it needs to be verified by multiple, trusted sources before we accept it as fact. [/20]

Interviewer: Abbi, Elliot, thank you.

questions.question: Abbi uses the term 'Liar's Dividend' to describe a situation where
options: A) people make money by creating fake videos of celebrities.
B) guilty people can avoid the truth by claiming real evidence is fake.
C) politicians are rewarded for being honest with the public.
D) companies pay a fine for using deepfake technology in ads.
questions.explanation: She explains it as when someone is caught on real video but claims it's a deepfake to escape accountability.

questions.question: What does Elliot say about the current state of deepfake detection?
options: A) It is now 100% effective thanks to new AI models.
B) It is getting harder because the AI is learning to avoid detection.
C) Most people can easily spot a deepfake without any software.
D) Only government agencies have access to detection tools.
questions.explanation: He calls it a 'back-and-forth' where AI improves its realism (like blinking) as soon as detection methods are found.

questions.question: Regarding the use of deepfakes in education, Abbi is concerned that
options: A) the technology is too expensive for most schools.
B) it might give students a distorted view of historical figures.
C) children will stop listening to their human teachers.
D) it will make history lessons too frightening for young kids.
questions.explanation: She says it's still 'fiction' and involves 'projecting our modern values onto the past'.

questions.question: Elliot believes that 'vishing' is particularly harmful because it
options: A) causes significant financial loss to major banks.
B) is impossible to trace back to the original hacker.
C) exploits the emotional trust we have in people we know.
D) interferes with the quality of international phone lines.
questions.explanation: He mentions the 'devastating' psychological impact of being betrayed by a familiar voice.

questions.question: Abbi suggests that in order to identify deepfakes, people should
options: A) only watch videos on official television channels.
B) look for technical glitches like blurry edges in the video.
C) consider the motivation and context of the person sharing the video.
D) ask a computer scientist to check every video they see.
questions.explanation: She argues for 'digital literacy' and looking for 'contextual clues' like the motive behind sharing.

questions.question: Elliot's final advice regarding digital information is to
options: A) stop using the internet for news altogether.
B) only trust videos that have been recorded on film.
C) treat all online content with the same skepticism as a rumor.
D) wait for a government official to confirm every news story.
questions.explanation: He says we must treat digital info as a rumor that needs verification from multiple sources.
